danger_signals.visual_form: Human henchmen, crocodiles, eagles, guard dogs appearing on screen
danger_signals.meaning: Combat threat; player must engage or avoid

danger_signals.visual_form: Crime boss character design, appears as final stage antagonist
Bullets on screen, flashing bullet indicator depletes
danger_signals.meaning: Ranged threat requiring evasion or armor protection

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy vehicles, bombs on driving stage roads
danger_signals.meaning: Collision = instant life loss in vehicle stages

danger_signals.visual_form: Annabelle Lane held by Gordon, narrative setup across stages
Ammo counter at screen top; NES version starts with less ammo than Famicom
danger_signals.meaning: Resource scarcity danger in shooting stages

safety_signals.visual_form: Chicken drumstick item drop from defeated enemies
safety_signals.meaning: Restores player health; safe to approach
Damage reduction; temporary invulnerability or protection

safety_signals.visual_form: Throwing knife, club, whip dropped by enemies
safety_signals.meaning: Enhanced melee combat capability; safe to engage

safety_signals.visual_form: Drops from defeated gunmen in rail-shooting stages

safety_signals.meaning: Destroys all on-screen enemies; temporary safety

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Bayou vigilante archetype; Crocodile Dundee-inspired character design
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Swamplands of Louisiana; Bayou; progression toward Bourbon Street and Gordon's estate

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Crime boss; New Orleans mobster; smuggling operations
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Human henchmen with varying appearances; specialized gunmen in shooting stages; vehicle-armed enforcers on roads
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Urban areas (Bourbon Street); Gordon's estate as final stronghold; crime-controlled territory progression

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Conspicuously absent; game emphasizes vigilante action outside law
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Bayou wilderness, swamps, grasslands, suburbs, urban streets — all treated as Gordon's domain

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Natural environment; crocodiles, eagles, animals as primary threats
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Billy moves through wilderness; combat-focused beat-em-up format

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mid-game — urban transition and vehicular threat
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Grasslands, freeway, suburbs; enemy vehicles on roads
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Jeep driving stages; time pressure; collision-based instant failure; different control scheme

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bourbon Street, urban environments; increased gunmen and armed enforcers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rail-shooting stages; Zapper-compatible first-person perspective; ammo management critical

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Godfather Gordon present; Annabelle Lane held captive; final boss arena
Easy mode: Billy saves Annabelle, comments on 'something lost'; Normal: standard ending; Bad ending: Annabelle leaves Billy; Kansai dialect ending: Billy and Annabelle speak in regional dialect
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Branching narrative based on difficulty and player actions; emotional/tonal variation in final scenes

world_state_to_visual.world_state: NES version — single ending, heightened difficulty
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: More aggressive enemy visuals; higher density of threats; narrower driving roads
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased enemy aggression, more health per enemy, reduced starting ammunition, jeep lacks health gauge

environmental_storytelling.example: Nine stages move Billy from swamplands → grasslands → freeway suburbs → urban Bourbon Street → crime boss estate. Visual geography tells story of infiltration into criminal territory.

environmental_storytelling.technique: Kidnapping motivation embedded in stage design
environmental_storytelling.example: Annabelle Lane's absence is constant across all stages; her presence only at final stage creates visual/narrative tension pulling player forward.

environmental_storytelling.technique: Regional visual difference (Famicom vs NES) as localization storytelling
environmental_storytelling.example: NES version: Annabelle wears denim shorts and midriff T-shirt (more revealing); enemy colors changed; suggests different tone/audience for Western market.

environmental_storytelling.technique: Gameplay format shift as territory change signal
environmental_storytelling.example: Beat-em-up → driving → rail shooting → beat-em-up creates stage variety that mirrors Billy's journey through different danger domains.
Early stages: animals (crocodiles, eagles, dogs); mid/late stages: human henchmen and gunmen. Shows transition from natural wilderness to urban organized crime.
NES adds DPCM announcer declaring title, Gordon laughing between stages — auditory faction presence strengthens villain characterization across regions.

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Performs punch, kick, jump kick; uses A/B button inputs for basic melee
Can draw/withdraw guns with Select button; flashing ammo indicator shows weapon active state
D-pad steers jeep, A/B launch grenades or shoot; single collision = instant life loss
First-person view; weapon drawn; must shoot gunmen and boss before ammo/health depleted
Attack Billy; more aggressive in NES version; greater health than Famicom version
Fire at Billy; drop ammo and health kits when defeated
Attack Billy in beat-em-up stages; signal wilderness territory
Laughs between stages (NES audio sample); holds Annabelle; ultimate combat encounter
Approach on narrow roads; player launches grenades/shoots; collision = instant failure

notes: Bayou Billy uses multi-genre stage design (beat-em-up, rail-shooting, driving) as world-state manifestation. The game communicates danger primarily through enemy presence, ammunition scarcity, and collision mechanics. Safety signals are explicitly item-based (pickups). Faction identity is implicit rather than symbolic: Billy is marked as outsider/vigilante by his progressive infiltration into organized crime territory (visual geography). The crime organization is communicated through enemy density increase and urban setting shift. NES localization changes (difficulty, visuals, audio) deliberately heightened danger perception vs. Famicom. Narrative world-state (kidnapping, rescue mission) is communicated through Annabelle's absence across stages and Gordon's presence at finale. Limited symbolic faction markers; identity relies on behavioral and environmental communication rather than colors/insignia.
sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - The Adventures of Bayou Billy
